primitive-types: Rust has several primitive types that form the building blocks of the language. Integer types include signed integers (i8-i128) and unsigned integers (u8-u128). Floating-point types are f32 and f64. Booleans use the bool type with values true or false. Characters are represented using char, which always represents a Unicode scalar value. Rust uses type inference with let bindings when you do not specify types explicitly, making code concise while maintaining strong type safety.
variables-immutability: In Rust, variables are immutable by default. You create immutable bindings using let statements. To create mutable variables, you must add the mut keyword. Rust also supports variable shadowing, where you can declare a new variable with the same name as an existing one, effectively replacing it. Constants are always immutable and can be declared with const. Understanding mutability rules is essential for writing safe Rust code.
data-structures: Rust provides several built-in data structures for organizing data. Arrays are fixed-size collections with compile-time length. Vectors are growable arrays that resize automatically. Tuples are fixed-length collections that can hold different types. Hash maps store key-value pairs with efficient lookups. Each data structure serves different use cases and has specific ownership and borrowing rules that must be followed to avoid compilation errors.
module-system: Rust's module system allows you to organize code into modules and crates. Use statements bring items from one module into another. The pub keyword controls visibility of functions, structs, and modules. Crates are the fundamental units of compilation. You can create libraries or executables as crates. Understanding visibility and module organization is crucial for building maintainable Rust projects with clear separation of concerns.
ownership: Ownership is Rust's most unique feature for memory safety without garbage collection. Each value has exactly one owner at any time. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped. Move semantics mean that assigning a value to another variable transfers ownership. Stack and heap memory are managed differently. Rust enforces ownership rules at compile time, preventing dangling pointers and use-after-free bugs that plague other languages.
borrowing: Borrowing allows you to use a value without taking ownership. You create immutable references with & or mutable references with &mut. The borrow checker ensures memory safety by enforcing borrowing rules at compile time. You can have multiple immutable references or exactly one mutable reference at any time, never both. References must not outlive the data they point to. Understanding references is essential for writing flexible and efficient Rust code.
slices: Slices are views into sequences of data without taking ownership. String slices (&str) are immutable views into UTF-8 strings. Slices use slice notation syntax [start..end] to view portions of arrays or vectors. They work with any contiguous sequence of elements. Slices are always non-owning and provide safe access to borrowed data. Understanding slices enables efficient string manipulation and array operations without memory allocation.
lifetimes: Lifetimes ensure that references are valid for as long as they are used. Annotations like 'a, 'static mark how long a reference must live. The borrow checker validates lifetime constraints at compile time. Lifetimes prevent dangling references by ensuring borrowed data outlives its borrows. Functions often need lifetime annotations when returning references. Understanding lifetimes is crucial for writing code that safely works with borrowed data.
results: Rust uses the Result<T, E> enum for fallible operations. It has two variants: Ok(T) for success and Err(E) for errors. Result forces explicit error handling, preventing ignored errors. The type parameter E represents the error type. Results can be chained and propagated. Error variants allow pattern matching and custom error types. Understanding Result is essential for writing robust Rust programs that handle failure gracefully.
options: The Option<T> enum represents values that may or may not exist. It has two variants: Some(T) when a value exists and None when it does not. Option replaces null and nil to eliminate null pointer exceptions. You handle Option with match expressions, if let patterns, or helper methods. Working with Option requires pattern matching or unwrapping, ensuring you handle absent values explicitly rather than implicitly.
error-handling: Rust provides several patterns for working with Options and Results. The question mark operator ? propagates errors from fallible functions. The unwrap() method panics when a value is None or Err. Match expressions exhaustively handle all variants. The if let pattern allows concise conditional handling. Methods like map, and_then, and unwrap_or provide functional alternatives. Mastering these patterns is essential for elegant and safe error handling in Rust.
custom-errors: Rust allows you to define your own error types for better error reporting. Custom errors typically implement the Error trait. You can use the thiserror or anyhow crates for ergonomic error definitions. Error types can contain context about what went wrong. The From trait enables automatic conversion of error types. Custom errors help callers understand failures and respond appropriately. Well-designed error types improve API usability and debuggability.
functions: Functions in Rust are defined with the fn keyword and return types. Methods are functions defined in impl blocks for specific types. The self keyword represents the instance in methods. Functions can have multiple parameters and named return values. Rust functions use currying through tuples and closure syntax. Understanding function signatures, method calls, and associated functions is fundamental to Rust programming.
structs: Structs let you create custom data types to group related data. Regular structs have named fields, tuple structs have unnamed indexed fields, and unit structs have no fields. Structs require the struct keyword followed by a name and brace-delimited fields. You create instances struct Name { field1: type1, field2: type2 }. Structs can also have methods and implementations. Understanding structs is essential for creating domain-specific data models.
enums: Enums define types that can be one of several variants. Each variant can carry different data. Enums in Rust are extremely powerful and often used for data modeling. Pattern matching with match ensures exhaustive handling of all variants. The if let syntax allows concise handling of specific variants. Enums plus pattern matching are common Rust idioms for expressing complex state and handling different cases safely.
traits: Traits define shared behavior across types in Rust. They specify what methods and functions a type must implement. You define impl blocks to provide trait implementations for types. Traits can have default implementations that types can choose to override. Trait bounds constrain generics to types that implement certain traits. Understanding traits is essential for writing generic, reusable Rust code with clear interfaces.
generics: Generics allow you to write code that works with multiple types. Generic types can be used in structs, enums, and functions. Type parameters specify what concrete types will be used later. Constraints limit which types can be used with generics. You can have multiple generic parameters with different constraints. Generics enable type-safe, reusable abstractions without code duplication. Understanding generics is crucial for writing flexible Rust code.
lifetimes-advanced: Advanced lifetime concepts include elision rules that simplify lifetime annotations. Functions can have generic lifetimes that relate multiple references. Life spans can vary and are represented with different lifetime parameters. Multiple lifetimes allow you to express complex borrowing relationships. Lifetime bounds specify that one lifetime must outlive another. Mastering these concepts enables writing safe, flexible functions that work with borrowed data.
concurrency: Rust provides safe concurrency through std::thread and channels. You create spawned threads to execute code in parallel. The Send trait marks types that can be transferred between threads. The Sync trait marks types that are safe to reference across threads. Channels enable message passing between threads. Shared pointers like Arc allow reference-counted shared ownership. Understanding concurrency is crucial for building performant concurrent Rust applications.
smart-pointers: Smart pointers are types that implement the Deref and Drop traits. Box creates a single-ownership heap allocation. Rc enables reference-counted shared ownership, Arc for thread-safe sharing. Cell and RefCell provide interior mutability for owned data. You can implement custom smart pointers for specific use cases. Smart pointers combine flexibility with Rust's ownership guarantees, enabling safe memory sharing and custom ownership models.
macros: Macros allow code generation and metaprogramming in Rust. macro_rules! defines declarative macros that match patterns and generate code. Derived macros with attributes like derive auto-generate trait implementations. Macros can reduce boilerplate and express domain-specific syntax. They are expanded before normal compilation. Understanding macros enables writing concise, reusable code patterns. Start with macro_rules! and progress to procedural macros for advanced use cases.
